answer:
## Câu trả lời thẳng: nên depend fork hay tự implement?

Tôi sẽ trả lời theo đúng 4 phần bạn yêu cầu, dựa trên corpus đã verify (kèm các verdict bị refute/uncertain — phản ánh trung thực).

---

## 1. libghostty forks: ngon hay liability để depend?

**Verdict thẳng: cả ba fork đều là liability ở mức độ khác nhau. wiedymi và daiimus là proof-of-concept, KHÔNG dùng được làm production dependency. Chỉ Lakr233/libghostty-spm là đáng cân nhắc nghiêm túc — nhưng vẫn mang single-maintainer bus factor và một "ABI-instability tax" cố hữu từ upstream.**

Phân loại từng fork:

- **wiedymi/ghostty `custom-io`** — 1 commit, frozen từ tháng 3/2026, 3 stars, không có resize callback, không CI. Đây là quyết định bạn đã chọn ban đầu, và nó là fork **yếu nhất** để depend trực tiếp.
  - Điểm sáng đáng kể: nó CÓ production evidence thật. **VVTerm** (App Store, native SSH client của Vivy Technologies, GPL-3.0) thực sự ship dùng fork này — `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md` của họ ghi rõ nguồn là `wiedymi/ghostty`, pin vào commit `268a0a9` trên branch custom-io ([github.com/vivy-company/vvterm](https://github.com/vivy-company/vvterm), [apps.apple.com VVTerm](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/vvterm-native-ssh-client/id6757482822)). Nhưng VVTerm chỉ có 4 ratings — đây là "early-stage production", **không phải** battle-tested.
  - ⚠️ Cảnh báo refute: corpus ban đầu claim wiedymi có "weekly CI cron `23 6 * * 1` tự build release" — **điều này SAI hoàn toàn**. Cron đó không tồn tại trong repo; pipeline release bị hard-gate `github.repository_owner == 'ghostty-org'` nên inert trên mọi fork; repo có **zero releases**. Đừng tin fork này tự maintain.

- **daiimus/ghostty `ios-external-backend`** — 1 commit, 0 stars, không CI, không XCFramework artifact, không Swift wrapper. `External.zig` (470 lines) hoàn chỉnh hơn wiedymi về mặt code (có resize callback, unit test cho linefeed), nhưng vẫn chỉ là prototype. Loại.

- **Lakr233/libghostty-spm** — fork **duy nhất** đáng đánh giá:
  - Weekly CI thật (`build.yml`, cron `23 6 * * 1`) tự track upstream tag, build XCFramework cho tất cả Apple targets, lipo-merge, `swift test`, publish release. 46 stars, 31 forks, 3 release trong 1 tháng.
  - Có Swift wrapper hoàn chỉnh: `InMemoryTerminalSession` map **chính xác** vào use-case host-owned-PTY của bạn — `write: @Sendable (Data) -> Void` để gửi keystroke về network, `receive(_ data: Data)` để push PTY bytes vào terminal, full iOS UIKit input + IME + accessory bar + Metal display link.
  - Patch `0003-prebuilt-framedata` (1720 lines) trông đáng sợ nhưng đã được verify **confirmed**: ~1700 lines đó là binary blob (animation `ghostty +boo`), code delta thật chỉ ~20 dòng build-script. **Không** ảnh hưởng VT parser/renderer.

**Liability chung của CẢ BA fork (load-bearing):** upstream `ghostty.h` ghi verbatim: *"This isn't meant to be a general purpose embedding API (yet)... The only consumer of this API is the macOS app."* Không có ABI-stability guarantee, không có tagged libghostty release. Bất kỳ Zig-side change nào ở upstream đều có thể phá C ABI mà không cần semver. Với Lakr233, CI sẽ *phát hiện* breakage (`git apply --check` fail) nhưng bạn vẫn có thể nhận xchframework hỏng cho đến khi maintainer tự rebase 5–8 patch (`0002` là unified diff, các patch khác là sed/Python script dễ vỡ).

> Lưu ý thêm: official ghostty-org đang phát triển **libghostty-vt** (C ABI riêng cho VT engine), nhưng header `vt.h` tự cảnh báo *"incomplete, work-in-progress API... definitely going to change"*, iOS **không phải** target được support, và build cần Zig toolchain + iOS cross-compile chưa có doc. Chưa sẵn sàng.

---

## 2. Tự implement có "không phức tạp lắm" không?

**Trả lời thẳng: phụ thuộc CHÍ MẠNG vào việc bạn tự viết NỬA NÀO. Tách đôi rạch ròi:**

### Nửa A — VT PARSING / STATE: KHÓ. Đừng tự viết từ đầu.

- **Phần dễ gây ngộ nhận:** state machine Paul Williams (14 states, 14 actions) chỉ ~900 LOC (SwiftTerm `EscapeSequenceParser.swift` = 898 LOC). Viết trong 1–2 tuần. **Đây KHÔNG phải bottleneck.**
- **Phần thật sự khó (85% công sức):** terminal-state layer. Mọi lib trưởng thành đều hội tụ về **6.800–10.000+ LOC** sau nhiều năm fix bug (SwiftTerm `Terminal.swift` = 6.821 LOC; xterm.js `InputHandler.ts` = 3.694 LOC). Long-tail correctness gồm:
  - SGR truecolor có bẫy ambiguity colon-vs-semicolon (`38:2:R:G:B` vs `38;2;R;G;B` — semicolon trùng delimiter, không có heuristic thì parse sai).
  - DEC private modes: alt-screen 1049, bracketed paste 2004, 4 biến thể mouse (1000/1002/1003/1006), DECCKM cursor key (vim phụ thuộc), autowrap.
  - Scroll regions (DECSTBM) tương tác với cursor movement + origin mode — đây là category vttest fail đầu tiên ở mọi parser DIY (xem Microsoft Terminal PR #3628).
  - G0/G1 charset + VT100 line-drawing (vim/tmux borders **bắt buộc**).
  - OSC 52 clipboard (essential cho remote vim qua SSH), OSC 0/2/7.
  - Kitty keyboard protocol (nvim phân biệt Ctrl-M vs Enter) — ~900 LOC encoder.
  - Reflow-on-resize.
  - **Unicode width / grapheme clusters — món khó nhất, chưa lib nào ship giải đúng 100% tính đến 2026** ([Mitchell Hashimoto on grapheme clusters](https://mitchellh.com/writing/grapheme-clusters-in-terminals), [jeffquast 2025 survey](https://www.jeffquast.com/post/state-of-terminal-emulation-2025/)). Riêng món này 2–4 engineer-weeks.

**Bằng chứng "không đơn giản":** Ghostty được Mitchell Hashimoto (systems engineer kỳ cựu) làm từ 2021, release 12/2024 — ~3 năm cho "chỉ một terminal emulator". xterm.js active từ ~2014, nhiều full-time contributor, *vẫn* còn open grapheme bug.

**Effort DIY nửa A:** good-enough subset = 12–16 engineer-weeks; vttest-clean = 24–32 engineer-weeks. → **Trap. Dùng lib trưởng thành.**

### Nửa B — RENDERING (Metal): VỪA SỨC. Đây là phần bạn NÊN tự viết.

- Failure mode là **visual và rõ ràng** (sai màu, thiếu glyph, lệch pixel), fixable trong 1 sprint — khác với correctness bug ngầm của parsing.
- Có ít nhất 2 reference Swift/Metal đọc line-by-line được: SwiftTerm `MetalTerminalRenderer` (2.763 LOC, đầy đủ) và Spectty `TerminalMetalRenderer` (570 LOC, skeleton sạch nhưng thiếu fallback/CJK/emoji).
- **CoreText gánh hết phần font khó:** `CTLineCreateWithAttributedString` **tự động** trigger system font cascade cho CJK/emoji/box-drawing mà **không cần** config `kCTFontCascadeListAttribute` — đã verify **confirmed** với Apple docs ([Core Text Programming Guide](https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/StringsTextFonts/Conceptual/CoreText_Programming/Overview/Overview.html), WWDC 2012 Session 226). Không cần HarfBuzz cho coding use-case.
- Truecolor/256-color trivial: chỉ là `mix(bg, fg, glyphAlpha)` 1 dòng trong fragment shader. Cursor/blink/selection là colored quad. Damage tracking per-row cache là pattern chuẩn.

**Effort DIY nửa B:** Metal full production = **6–10 engineer-weeks**; CoreText-only (non-GPU) v1 = **2–3 engineer-weeks** (chấp nhận degrade trên btop/fast-scroll ở 120Hz ProMotion).

> ⚠️ Cảnh báo refute quan trọng cho Spectty: claim "Spectty silently drops CJK/emoji to blank" đã bị **refuted** — thực ra fallback xảy ra ở layer `CTLineCreateWithAttributedString`/ShaperCache nên CJK/emoji **được** render đúng. Đừng vì claim sai đó mà loại CoreText cascade.

---

## 3. Bảng so sánh các path

| Path | Chất lượng render | Coding-feature coverage | Toolchain / maintenance burden | Effort (eng-weeks) | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(a) libghostty fork** (Lakr233) | Cao nhất (Ghostty Metal pipeline, prebuilt framedata) | Đầy đủ nhất (Kitty kb + graphics + Sixel + truecolor + mouse, InMemorySession sẵn) | **Cao & không kiểm soát được** — single maintainer; rebase 5–8 patch mỗi Ghostty release; ABI có thể vỡ bất kỳ minor nào | **1–2** (nhanh nhất ra app) | **Cao** — bus factor + ABI instability tax + Zig build system nếu maintainer bỏ |
| **(b) libvterm + own Metal** | Bạn tự chủ (vừa sức) | Tốt NHƯNG **thiếu Kitty keyboard** (nvim mất phân biệt Ctrl-M/Enter) + wcwidth table 2007 (grapheme cũ) | Thấp — vendor 1 file C99, bridging header Xcode chuẩn. *Nhưng* bản standalone "not used by neovim", bản được fix nằm bundled trong neovim/neovim (khó vendor) | 3–5 ngày integrate + ~2 tuần patch Kitty + 6–10 render | Trung bình — C ổn định, nhưng gap feature tự gánh |
| **(c) SwiftTerm engine + own Metal renderer** | Bạn tự chủ (vừa sức, đúng phần nên tự viết) | Đầy đủ ở nửa parsing (Kitty kb 904 LOC, Kitty graphics, Sixel, OSC 52, grapheme qua Swift String, esctest-validated) | **Thấp nhất** — pure Swift, SPM-native, iOS 14+, Foundation-only core, không FFI/Zig/Rust | **6–10** (chỉ tốn cho render + integration; parsing free) | **Thấp** — MIT, deployed thật (Secure Shellfish, La Terminal, CodeEdit), active 2026-05 |
| **(d) SwiftTerm wholesale** (dùng cả TerminalView) | Có Metal renderer sẵn **nhưng**: `useMetalRenderer = false` mặc định + maintainer ghi "Experimental GPU path, still evolving"; default vẫn là CoreGraphics path | Đầy đủ | Thấp nhất, nhưng bạn không own render → khó tùy biến UX/perf | **1–2** | Thấp về dependency; nhưng Metal path chưa production-default → có thể phải tự nâng |
| **(e) Full DIY** (cả A + B) | Bạn tự chủ | Tự gánh **toàn bộ** long-tail correctness | Không có dependency, nhưng bạn TRỞ THÀNH maintainer của VT engine | **18–26** good-enough; **30–42** vttest-clean | **Rất cao** — long-tail VT bug là cái bẫy nhiều tháng |

**Hiệu chỉnh trung thực cho (c)/(d) — load-bearing:**
- Claim "SwiftTerm Terminal.swift Foundation-only ⇒ compilation unit sạch trên iOS" đã bị **refuted**. `Package.swift` gom cả `Apple/`, `iOS/`, `Mac/` vào **cùng một SPM target** trên Apple platforms — nên build iOS của module SwiftTerm **có** link UIKit/Metal/MetalKit/CoreGraphics/SwiftUI. Symbol không re-export (không `@_exported`) nhưng vẫn được compile vào module. **Muốn chỉ lấy VT engine Foundation-pure, bạn phải fork SwiftTerm và tách target** thành headless-core product + view-layer product. Đây là chi phí thật cho path (c), cộng ~vài ngày.
- `HeadlessTerminal` bị guard `#if !os(iOS)` → **không** dùng trên iOS. Nhưng không sao: entry point đúng là class `Terminal` trực tiếp + `terminal.feed(buffer:)` + `TerminalDelegate`, platform-agnostic, đúng pattern network-PTS của bạn.
- SwiftTerm grapheme: **uncertain** — CJK width + emoji (ZWJ/skin-tone/flag/VS16) là correct & test-backed (port từ Ghostty test suite); nhưng bảng `combining` trong `Utilities.swift` là wcwidth cũ (~Unicode 9), được che bởi `UnicodeScalar.properties.generalCategory` của Apple runtime. Với use-case btop + nvim + CJK filenames thực tế → **ổn**.

Rust options (alacritty / wezterm) tôi xếp dưới (c)/(d): thêm Cargo + iOS cross-compile + Swift FFI bridge. Compile `alacritty_terminal` cho `aarch64-apple-ios` là **uncertain** (link-time `openpty` chưa verify; `tty::new()` chỉ fail runtime — nhưng client của bạn không gọi tty nên về lý thuyết ok). wezterm-term **không** publish lên crates.io → phải vendor cả monorepo. Toolchain tax không đáng cho một app pure-Swift.

---

## 4. Khuyến nghị dứt khoát

**Chọn path (c): SwiftTerm `Terminal` engine (nửa A) + tự viết Metal renderer (nửa B).**

Đây chính là "sweet spot" bạn framing đúng, và nó tôn trọng nguyên tắc *"đừng depend lib non nếu tự làm được ở mức hợp lý"*:

1. **Bỏ wiedymi/ghostty.** Nó là fork yếu nhất, 1-commit orphaned, và corpus đã refute việc nó "tự maintain qua CI". Production evidence duy nhất (VVTerm) quá mỏng (4 ratings). Quyết định ban đầu của bạn nên đảo lại.

2. **Không tự viết nửa A (VT parsing/state).** Đây *đúng* là loại lib "khó-nhưng-đã-có-bản-mature". Tự viết = 12–32 engineer-weeks rơi vào long-tail correctness trap (grapheme, scroll region, SGR ambiguity). Không hợp lý để DIY. Dùng SwiftTerm `Terminal` — pure Swift, MIT, esctest-validated, deployed thật, không toolchain tax.

3. **Tự viết nửa B (Metal renderer).** Đây *đúng* là phần "vừa sức, tự kiểm soát được" — failure mode visual, có 2 reference Swift đọc được, CoreText gánh font cascade. 6–10 weeks cho production, hoặc ship CoreText-only v1 trong 2–3 weeks rồi nâng cấp. Bạn own UX/perf/ProMotion 120Hz, không phụ thuộc Metal path "experimental" của SwiftTerm.

4. **Chi phí thực tế cần budget:** fork SwiftTerm để **tách SPM target** (headless VT core tách khỏi UIKit/Metal layer) — vì upstream gom chung target trên Apple platforms (claim Foundation-only đã bị refute ở mức module). Đây là fork *nhẹ, một lần*, không phải gánh nặng rebase liên tục như Ghostty patch — bạn chỉ pin version và đồng bộ khi cần. Nếu lười tách: cứ import cả module và bỏ qua view layer, chấp nhận binary link thêm vài Apple framework (không re-export, không ảnh hưởng API surface).

**Nếu muốn ship cực nhanh để validate sản phẩm trước:** dùng path (d) — SwiftTerm wholesale với CoreGraphics renderer (mặc định, production-stable) — 1–2 weeks. Rồi migrate sang path (c) (tự viết Metal) khi cần perf cho btop/fast-scroll. Đừng dùng Metal-path-of-SwiftTerm làm production vì nó vẫn `useMetalRenderer = false` + maintainer gắn nhãn "experimental".

**Path (a) Lakr233 chỉ hợp lý nếu:** bạn ưu tiên time-to-market tuyệt đối (1–2 weeks), chấp nhận single-maintainer bus factor + rebase tax mỗi Ghostty release, và pin chặt vào một `storage.X.Y.Z` release tag content-addressed bằng checksum. Nó cho chất lượng render cao nhất ngay lập tức — nhưng đánh đổi bằng việc *không bao giờ thật sự own* được stack khi upstream churn.

**Tóm tắt một dòng:** Dùng SwiftTerm cho phần khó-không-nên-tự-làm (VT state), tự viết Metal renderer cho phần vừa-sức-nên-own (rendering). Bỏ wiedymi. Coi Lakr233 là fallback nhanh nếu cần ship gấp, không phải lựa chọn dài hạn.

corpus.summary: All three libghostty forks are personal, single-maintainer projects with zero upstream coordination. None have tagged C-ABI releases; none have stability commitments. The forks diverge at the same conceptual point — replacing Ghostty's exec/PTY backend with an external-IO backend — but differ dramatically in maintenance investment and production readiness. Lakr233/libghostty-spm is by far the most credible option: it has a weekly CI pipeline that auto-tracks upstream tagged releases, ships pre-built XCFramework slices for all Apple targets, has 46 stars and 31 forks, and carries a full Swift wrapper with iOS UIKit input, Metal rendering, and an InMemory session abstraction purpose-built for the host-owned-PTY use-case. The wiedymi/ghostty custom-io and daiimus/ghostty ios-external-backend forks are both frozen at a single commit from March 2026, show zero community adoption, and carry unquantified rebase debt. For a production app, none of these forks is zero-risk, but Lakr233 is categorically less risky than the other two and is the only one worth evaluating seriously. The core liability that applies to all of them is that upstream ghostty.h carries an explicit "not a general-purpose embedding API (yet)" disclaimer with no ABI-stability guarantee, meaning any upstream ghostty minor release can break the custom-IO patch at the C level.

points.claim: wiedymi/ghostty custom-io is a single orphaned commit, frozen since March 2026, with no rebase cadence
points.detail: The branch contains exactly one commit (91fe505, 2026-03-08), authored by wiedymi, on top of ghostty main at ghostty 1.3.2-dev. The repo has 3 stars, 5 forks, and was last pushed 2026-05-02 — no activity since the initial commit. The custom-IO surface is implemented as a new termio backend called `Callback` (src/termio/Callback.zig, ~170 lines). The C API adds two functions to ghostty.h: `ghostty_surface_feed_data` (push bytes from host into the terminal) and `ghostty_surface_set_write_callback` (register a callback that receives keystroke bytes to send back). Activation requires setting `use_custom_io: bool = true` on the embedded surface options struct. The implementation is plausible but minimal — no resize-callback, no process-exit signal, no thread-safety documentation for the callback invocation context. VVTerm, which reportedly uses this fork, does not appear to be a public GitHub repo, so there is no evidence of real production deployment.

points.claim: daiimus/ghostty ios-external-backend is also a single orphaned commit from March 2026, but has a more complete External backend than wiedymi
points.detail: The branch is one commit (21c7173, 2026-03-10) on ghostty 1.3.0. It has 0 stars, 0 forks, and has not been updated since. The implementation is more thorough than wiedymi: it introduces a dedicated `src/termio/External.zig` (470 lines) with a proper `GHOSTTY_BACKEND_EXTERNAL` enum value in ghostty.h, write and resize callback types as part of `ghostty_surface_config_s`, and a `ghostty_surface_write_output()` function to push PTY bytes in. The AGENTS.md documents the iOS build pipeline (zig build -Demit-xcframework=true) and explicitly says issues are tracked on daiimus/geistty, not upstream. However there is no geistty repo visible, no iOS demo app in the tree, no CI, and no XCFramework artifact. The External.zig has unit tests for linefeed expansion, which is a positive signal for completeness, but overall this is a proof-of-concept, not a shippable dependency.

points.claim: Lakr233/libghostty-spm is the only fork with active automated maintenance — a weekly CI pipeline tracks and rebuilds against the latest upstream tagged release
points.detail: The repo has a GitHub Actions workflow (`build.yml`) on a weekly cron (`23 6 * * 1` = Monday 6:23 UTC) that auto-detects the latest semver tag from ghostty-org/ghostty, clones it, applies the local Patches/ghostty/ directory, builds per-target static libraries for aarch64-macos, x86_64-macos, aarch64-ios, aarch64-ios-simulator, x86_64-ios-simulator, aarch64-ios-macabi, x86_64-ios-macabi, lipo-merges them, assembles a GhosttyKit.xcframework, runs swift test, then publishes a new GitHub release. The latest release (1.2.3, published 2026-06-01) was built from upstream v1.3.1 (sha 332b2ae). Package.swift uses a versioned binaryTarget pointing at the release ZIP with a hardcoded checksum, giving consumers reproducible builds. There is 1 open issue and 31 forks — meaningful for a 3-month-old package.

points.claim: Lakr233's host-managed-IO patch (0002) is functionally equivalent to daiimus's External backend and Wiedymi's Callback backend, but is structurally cleaner and documented in patch form for auditability
points.detail: Patch 0002 (518 lines) adds `src/termio/HostManaged.zig` (136 lines) — a termio backend with a `write` callback (fn(?*anyopaque, [*]const u8, usize)), an optional `resize` callback (fn(?*anyopaque, u16, u16, u32, u32)), and `ghostty_surface_write_buffer` / `ghostty_surface_process_exit` C-API functions. The `ghostty_surface_config_s` gains a `backend` enum field (`EXEC` vs `HOST_MANAGED`), `receive_userdata`, `receive_buffer`, and `receive_resize` callback pointers. The patch is a standard unified diff applied via `git apply`, idempotent (apply-patches.sh checks `--check --reverse` before re-applying). Patches 0004-0008 are shell scripts that handle iOS-specific quirks: kqueue panic suppression, CGSSetWindowBackgroundBlurRadius removal for App Store compliance, IOSurface ±1px tolerance fix, Metal texture storage changes, inspector disabling. These represent real iOS integration work that neither wiedymi nor daiimus surfaces in usable form. Critically, 0003 (prebuilt-framedata, 1720 lines) pre-computes Metal frame data at build time — a significant rendering optimization whose correctness has not been independently verified.

points.claim: Lakr233/libghostty-spm ships a real Swift wrapper (GhosttyTerminal) with full iOS UIKit input, IME, keyboard accessory bar, Metal display link, and an InMemoryTerminalSession abstraction that directly maps to the host-owned-PTY pattern
points.detail: GhosttyTerminal (~40 Swift files) provides: TerminalSurfaceView (SwiftUI representable for both AppKit and UIKit), UITerminalView with full UITextInput conformance, hardware key translation via UIPress, sticky modifier state for the input accessory bar (Esc, Tab, arrows, Ctrl/Alt/Cmd), IME/marked text via preedit, touch scrolling with momentum via CADisplayLink, and Catalyst pointer/mouse support. InMemoryTerminalSession is the host-IO bridge: it holds a `write: @Sendable (Data) -> Void` (bytes to send to SSH) and `resize: @Sendable (InMemoryTerminalViewport) -> Void`, registers C callbacks for `receive_buffer` and `receive_resize`, and exposes `receive(_ data: Data)` for pushing PTY output from the network into the terminal. The `TerminalSessionBackend` enum has `.exec` and `.inMemory(InMemoryTerminalSession)` cases. The MobileGhosttyApp example demonstrates the full iOS integration. This is the most complete ready-to-use surface of any of the three forks.

points.claim: All three forks share the same fundamental liability: upstream ghostty.h explicitly disclaims being a general-purpose embedding API, has no ABI stability guarantee, and has no versioned releases — meaning the host-managed-IO C interface can break on any upstream commit
points.detail: The upstream ghostty.h header comment reads verbatim: 'This isn't meant to be a general purpose embedding API (yet) so there hasn't been documentation or example work beyond that. The only consumer of this API is the macOS app.' There are no tagged libghostty releases on ghostty-org/ghostty (the tag list shows only application releases like v1.3.1). The types in ghostty.h are documented as 'must be kept in sync with their Zig counterparts. Any changes to these types MUST have an associated Zig change' — i.e., Zig-side changes break the C ABI without a semver contract. For the host-managed-IO patches specifically, the patch context lines in Lakr233's 0002 patch will drift against upstream Surface.zig on every upstream refactor. The current patch targets v1.3.1; whether it will apply cleanly to v1.4.x is unknown. Lakr233's weekly CI will detect this breakage (a failing `git apply --check` exits non-zero), but it means downstream consumers can receive a broken xcframework until Lakr233 manually updates the patch.

points.claim: Lakr233's license is MIT (personal copyright, not a ghostty-org fork license), which is clean for production use; wiedymi and daiimus retain the upstream MIT license with Ghostty contributor attribution
points.detail: Lakr233/libghostty-spm: 'MIT License, Copyright (c) 2026 @Lakr233'. wiedymi and daiimus both carry the upstream 'MIT License, Copyright (c) 2024 Mitchell Hashimoto, Ghostty contributors'. All three are permissive MIT and present no licensing obstacle to commercial use. GhosttyTheme (485 themes) also MIT from iTerm2-Color-Schemes. The binary XCFramework distributed by Lakr233 is a build artifact of upstream ghostty source (MIT) plus the patches — no additional license obligations.

points.claim: The honest verdict: wiedymi and daiimus are proof-of-concept forks unsuitable as production dependencies; Lakr233/libghostty-spm is credible as a starting point but carries single-maintainer bus factor and an inherent ABI-instability tax from upstream
points.detail: wiedymi: 1 commit, 3 stars, no CI, no iOS app demo, no resize callback, last touched May 2026 (a push to main, not custom-io). Effectively abandoned. daiimus: 1 commit, 0 stars, no CI, no XCFramework artifacts, no Swift wrapper, last touched March 2026. Effectively a prototype. Lakr233: weekly CI, 46 stars, 31 forks, 3 tagged releases in the past month (1.2.1, 1.2.2, 1.2.3), full Swift wrapper with iOS UIKit, Metal rendering, InMemoryTerminalSession. The real risk matrix for Lakr233: (a) single maintainer — if Lakr233 stops maintaining, you inherit a forked Zig build system; (b) patch rebase burden — each upstream ghostty release may require manually updating 5-8 patches (0002 is a unified diff, others are fragile sed/Python scripts); (c) 0003 prebuilt-framedata (1720-line patch) introduces rendering changes whose correctness against Ghostty's full rendering pipeline is unverified by tests. If you do depend on Lakr233, pin to a specific `storage.X.Y.Z` release tag (the binary XCFramework is content-addressed by checksum), and monitor upstream for patch-breaking refactors. The alternative — implement a thin VT state machine yourself (libvterm or SwiftTerm) and own the renderer — eliminates all three risks at the cost of 2-4 weeks of initial work versus depending on a fork that could silently break on the next Ghostty release.

corpus.effort_estimate: Integrating Lakr233/libghostty-spm: 1-2 weeks initial integration (Swift Package dependency, configure InMemoryTerminalSession, wire network bytes to receive(), wire keystrokes to sendKeyEvent()/sendText(), test with neovim/tmux/btop). Ongoing: monitor weekly CI for patch failures; budget ~4-8h per upstream Ghostty minor release for patch rebase if needed. Forking wiedymi or daiimus instead: add 2-4 weeks to produce a working iOS XCFramework from scratch using the patch pattern, plus the same ongoing rebase cost. Building your own VT state layer: libvterm C + Metal renderer = 3-6 weeks for production-quality truecolor + mouse + kitty keyboard; SwiftTerm headless + Metal renderer = 2-4 weeks (pure Swift, no FFI tax). The Lakr233 path is the fastest route to a working app but the highest ongoing maintenance risk per upstream churn.

open_questions: Is there any official ghostty-org roadmap item or tracking issue for formalizing the libghostty embedding API with stability guarantees? If yes, that changes the risk calculus entirely.
Does Lakr233's 0003-prebuilt-framedata patch affect truecolor rendering correctness for 256-color and true-color (24-bit) sequences used by btop, lazygit, neovim colorschemes?

verifications.claim: Lakr233's 0003-prebuilt-framedata.patch (1720 lines) does not introduce rendering regressions — this has not been independently tested against a vttest suite

verdict.claim: Lakr233's 0003-prebuilt-framedata.patch (1720 lines) does not introduce rendering regressions — this has not been independently tested against a vttest suite
verdict.evidence:
The claim has two sub-parts, both of which are confirmed:

**Part 1 — "does not introduce rendering regressions"**

Direct inspection of [Lakr233/libghostty-spm](https://github.com/Lakr233/libghostty-spm/blob/main/Patches/ghostty/0003-prebuilt-framedata.patch) shows the patch is 1720 lines because the bulk of it is binary-encoded compressed data (the pre-built `framedata.compressed` file embedded in git's binary patch format). The code change is trivial: it replaces a build-time step that compiled `framegen` (a C tool in `src/build/framegen/main.c`) and ran it against ASCII art text frames for Ghostty's `+boo` easter-egg animation, with a reference to a pre-compiled `src/build/framegen/framedata.compressed`. The modified file is `src/build/GhosttyFrameData.zig`, which is build infrastructure only. The README's own "Trimmed Build" table corroborates: "Frame data generator: framedata.compressed shipped pre-built; framegen C tool dependency removed." No VT parser, no terminal state machine, no renderer code paths, no Metal shader code — no rendering regressions are possible from this change.

**Part 2 — "has not been independently tested against a vttest suite"**

The CI for libghostty-spm (`Script/test.sh`) runs only `xcodebuild` build compilation tests across platform schemes (macOS, iOS, Mac Catalyst). There is no vttest invocation, no terminal conformance test, and no xterm-compatibility suite. The `swift test` call in `build.sh` runs Swift unit tests, not external vttest harnesses. No vttest runs were found anywhere in the repository. This is independently verifiable — the [Ghostty upstream itself has open vttest failures](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/discussions/4047) (blinking text, VT52 mode, occasional screen update delays), and those are inherited by libghostty-spm unchanged. Neither the upstream nor the fork runs vttest in CI.

The 1720-line figure is misleading in context: it signals a large binary blob, not a large code change. The actual code delta is approximately 20 lines of build script.
verdict.correction: The claim is confirmed but contains a misleading implication. The patch's 1720-line size is almost entirely binary payload — the pre-built compressed animation frames for `ghostty +boo`. The actual code change is ~20 lines of build-script wiring that eliminates a compile-time C tool dependency. It is categorically a build-infrastructure change with zero effect on the VT parser, terminal state machine, or Metal renderer. Rendering regressions from this patch are architecturally impossible. The vttest-untested claim is accurate and applies to the full libghostty-spm surface: the test suite is build-only (xcodebuild compilation checks), with no conformance testing whatsoever. Known vttest gaps (VT52 mode, blinking text, intermittent screen updates) are inherited from upstream Ghostty, which itself does not run vttest in CI.

verifications.claim: The weekly CI at cron '23 6 * * 1' actually fires and produces a new release each week — verify by checking release timestamps across several weeks in the release history

verdict.claim: The weekly CI at cron '23 6 * * 1' in wiedymi/ghostty actually fires and produces a new release each week — verified by checking release timestamps across several weeks in the release history.
verdict.evidence:
Three independent facts refute the claim entirely:

1. THE CRON SCHEDULE DOES NOT EXIST. Exhaustive inspection of every workflow file in wiedymi/ghostty (.github/workflows/*.yml, 16 files total) found exactly two cron schedules: `0 0 * * *` in clean-artifacts.yml (daily artifact cleanup) and `0 0 * * 0` in update-colorschemes.yml (iTerm2 colorscheme update, Sundays). The schedule `23 6 * * 1` (Mondays at 06:23) is absent from every file in the fork. Source: GitHub API /repos/wiedymi/ghostty/contents/.github/workflows, confirmed per-file.

2. THE RELEASE PIPELINE IS OWNER-GATED AND INERT ON THE FORK. The release-tip.yml workflow — which is the only workflow capable of producing a release — has a hard guard on every substantive job: `github.repository_owner == 'ghostty-org'`. On wiedymi/ghostty the `setup` job evaluates this condition to false and the entire pipeline skips. This is confirmed in both the fork's copy and the upstream ghostty-org copy, which are identical in this section. Source: GitHub API /repos/wiedymi/ghostty/contents/.github/workflows/release-tip.yml (decoded base64).

3. ZERO RELEASES EXIST. The GitHub releases API for wiedymi/ghostty returns an empty array `[]`. There is no `tip` prerelease tag, no versioned release, no release at any timestamp — weekly or otherwise. Source: GET https://api.github.com/repos/wiedymi/ghostty/releases?per_page=30 → [].

4. FORK CHARACTERIZATION. wiedymi/ghostty has 3 stars, was created 2026-01-06, last pushed 2026-04-23, and has no custom CI or custom-io backend. The only iOS-specific code is NullPty (src/pty.zig lines 58–104), a compile stub that opens, reads, and writes nothing — explicitly commented as a temporary stopgap. Scheduled workflow runs visible via the Actions API are only housekeeping jobs (clean-artifacts success daily, vouch-sync-codeowners cancelled weekly). No build or release runs appear in the run history.
verdict.correction: The cron `23 6 * * 1` does not exist in wiedymi/ghostty or anywhere in the ghostty-org upstream. The fork produces no releases at all — the release pipeline (release-tip.yml) hard-codes `github.repository_owner == 'ghostty-org'` and is inert on any fork. The only scheduled workflows that actually fire on the fork are daily artifact cleanup and a weekly Sunday colorscheme updater, neither of which builds or publishes anything. The fork should be understood as a thin tracking mirror (3 stars, last active April 2026, no CI build runs, iOS support is a NullPty stub) — not a productized libghostty distribution. For the user's use-case (consuming a stable libghostty-vt C ABI on iOS/macOS), the correct upstream source is ghostty-org/ghostty itself, which does build and publish a ghostty-vt.xcframework on every merge to main via the same release-tip.yml (gated to `ghostty-org` owner, so it fires there).

verifications.claim: VVTerm (the app reportedly using wiedymi custom-io) exists and ships — could not find a public repo; if it ships on App Store, that is meaningful production evidence for wiedymi's approach

verdict.claim: VVTerm (the app reportedly using wiedymi custom-io) exists and ships — could not find a public repo; if it ships on App Store, that is meaningful production evidence for wiedymi's approach
verdict.evidence:
Every part of the original claim is now verifiable and confirmed, with the "could not find a public repo" portion being the only sub-claim that is wrong (the repo is fully public):

1. VVTerm ships on the App Store. The listing at https://apps.apple.com/us/app/vvterm-native-ssh-client/id6757482822 is live, the developer is Vivy Technologies, and version 2.2 was updated as of ~May 2026. It has 4 ratings at 5.0 stars.

2. The public repo is at https://github.com/vivy-company/vvterm — created 2026-01-07, 268 stars, 22 forks, last updated 2026-05-30, GPL-3.0 licensed. It is not private.

3. VVTerm explicitly attributes wiedymi/ghostty. The file THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md in the repo states verbatim: "Source: https://github.com/wiedymi/ghostty (fork of https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty)". The Vendor/libghostty directory contains a vendored binary xcframework with a VERSION file pinned to commit 268a0a9d761fb19673f05d28042488e2002300f2.

4. That pinned commit (268a0a9) is on the custom-io branch of wiedymi/ghostty, dated 2026-03-07, with message "iOS: showcase custom I/O callback backend". The custom-io branch has commits going back to 2026-01-06 ("Add callback backend for custom I/O (SSH clients, etc) — Add Callback.zig backend").

5. The wiedymi/ghostty fork itself is a live fork (3 stars, 5 forks) with the custom-io branch carrying iOS-specific additions on top of upstream ghostty. Its most recent upstream-tracking commits are from 2026-04-23, so it is being kept reasonably current with upstream.

Maturity caveats the claim does NOT make but are material for the user's decision:
- wiedymi/ghostty has 3 stars (extremely low visibility outside VVTerm's usage).
- The custom-io branch has only ~7 original commits beyond the upstream merge base; it is a small, focused patch, not a heavily tested library.
- VVTerm pins a pre-built xcframework binary (not building from source), meaning downstream consumers cannot easily track upstream changes or audit what is in the binary.
- The App Store app has only 4 ratings total — this is a very new, lightly-used app, so "production evidence" is real but thin. It does not constitute evidence of heavy battle-testing under diverse terminal workloads.
verdict.correction: The sub-claim "could not find a public repo" is wrong — the repo https://github.com/vivy-company/vvterm is fully public. Everything else in the original claim is confirmed: the app ships on the App Store, and the THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md file explicitly names wiedymi/ghostty as the libghostty source, with the vendored binary pinned to a specific custom-io branch commit (268a0a9, 2026-03-07). The claim that this is "meaningful production evidence" is directionally correct but should be qualified: VVTerm is a real, live, recently-updated app, but with only 4 App Store ratings it is early-stage production, not a heavily battle-tested signal.

corpus.summary: Building a correct VT state machine from scratch in Swift for vim/neovim/tmux/lazygit/btop/fzf is firmly in "multi-month trap" territory if you aim for vttest-clean correctness. The parser state machine itself (Paul Williams, 14 states, ~900 LOC) is the tractable part. The trap is the other 85% of the work: a 6,800-LOC terminal-state layer handling DEC private modes, SGR with colon/semicolon truecolor ambiguity, four mouse-protocol variants, grapheme/wide-char correctness, G0/G1 charset switching, scroll regions, alt-screen, reflow-on-resize, Kitty keyboard protocol, and a zoo of OSC codes. Every mature terminal library (SwiftTerm, xterm.js, wezterm, alacritty) converges on 10,000–20,000 LOC of state code after years of bug-fixing. The "good enough for coding" subset is reachable in roughly 8–12 engineer-weeks; vttest-clean robustness takes 20–30+. Unicode/grapheme is the single hardest long-tail item and remains unsolved even in shipped terminals as of 2026.

points.claim: The Paul Williams parser has 14 states and 14 action types — the raw state machine is ~900 LOC and tractable
points.detail: The canonical description at vt100.net/emu/dec_ansi_parser defines 14 states (ground, escape, escapeIntermediate, csiEntry, csiParam, csiIntermediate, csiIgnore, dcsEntry, dcsParam, dcsIntermediate, dcsPassthrough, dcsIgnore, oscString, sosPmApcString) and 14 actions (ignore, error, print, execute, clear, collect, param, escDispatch, csiDispatch, oscStart, oscPut, oscEnd, hook/put/unhook for DCS). SwiftTerm's EscapeSequenceParser.swift implementing exactly this is 898 LOC. alacritty/vte's lib.rs (the pure state machine) is 1,544 LOC; wezterm's vtparse/lib.rs is 1,165 LOC. All three are close to the same size. This layer is NOT the complexity bottleneck — it is well-understood, well-documented, and a Swift implementation can be completed in 1–2 weeks by one engineer. The real complexity is in the terminal-state layer that consumes the parser's output.

points.claim: The terminal-state layer (the hard part) is 6,800–10,000+ LOC per mature library and grows over years of bug-fixing
points.detail: Measured directly from source: SwiftTerm's Terminal.swift is 6,821 LOC (plus 1,649 for Buffer/BufferLine, 957 for Kitty keyboard, 403 for Sixel, 262 for CharSets = ~10,000 LOC core). wezterm's equivalent layer is terminalstate/mod.rs (2,766) + performer.rs (1,109) + mouse.rs (364) + kitty.rs (979) + screen.rs (1,155) = ~6,400 LOC just for the performer/state side, backed by wezterm-escape-parser's csi.rs (3,395) + osc.rs (2,000) = a further 5,400 LOC of semantic decoding. xterm.js InputHandler.ts alone is 3,694 LOC. These numbers reflect years of incremental correctness fixes, not a first-pass implementation. A fresh DIY implementation will start at roughly 2,000–3,000 LOC but grow toward these numbers as real-world bugs surface.

points.claim: SGR truecolor parsing has a real ambiguity trap: semicolons vs. colons, and 38;2 consumes subsequent parameters
points.detail: ECMA-48 specifies colon-separated sub-parameters (38:2:R:G:B), but xterm's historic implementation popularized semicolons (38;2;R;G;B). These semantically conflict because semicolons are also standard parameter delimiters, meaning a parser can't tell where the RGB triplet ends without heuristics. SwiftTerm's EscapeSequenceParser.swift explicitly notes this on line 8–11: 'The state machine has been extended to allow ":" in the CSI Param state to initiate a new parameter value. This is strictly not part of the spec, but necessary to parse the color scheme CSI [ 48:2:R:G:B m sequence which uses ":" instead of the more common ";".' The safe approach is to accept both separators, which requires a minor state-machine extension. SwiftTerm's SGR handler is at Terminal.swift:3614+ and runs ~400 LOC covering 16-color, 256-color (38;5;n), truecolor (38;2;r;g;b), and the full SGR attribute table.

points.claim: DEC private modes required for coding: alt-screen (1049), bracketed paste (2004), mouse (1000/1002/1003/1006), cursor keys, autowrap — each has edge cases
points.detail: SwiftTerm's Terminal.swift handles all of these. Key observations from the source: (1) Mouse encoding has four variants: X10 (1000), button (1002), any (1003), and SGR (1006) — each encodes coordinates differently and the emulator must track which is active and encode responses correctly. SwiftTerm tracks mouseMode + mouseProtocol as separate state variables. (2) Alt-screen (1049) saves/restores cursor position AND switches buffers; modes 1047 and 1049 behave slightly differently on clear. (3) DECSTBM (scroll regions) must interact correctly with cursor movement commands — Terminal.swift line 1012 shows the 'r' CSI handler. (4) Bracketed paste (2004) is straightforward but must not be forgotten — nvim relies on it. (5) Cursor key mode (DECCKM, mode 1) changes whether arrow keys send application or normal sequences — critical for vim. Each mode is ~5–30 LOC to implement but the interaction surface is large.

points.claim: Scroll regions (DECSTBM), cursor movement, and screen-relative operations are vttest's hardest category and remain buggy in many terminals
points.detail: The Zutty vttest results page documents that even a carefully built terminal (Zutty, VT520-level) passes all cursor-movement and scroll-region tests — but this required deliberate, correct implementation. Microsoft Terminal's PR #3628 ('Improve the VT cursor movement implementation') consolidated three different cursor movement code paths into one and fixed CNL/CPL not being constrained by DECSTBM margins. This is a representative example of the kind of correctness bug that surfaces late: movement commands must respect the active scroll region's top and bottom margins, and different commands have subtly different interaction rules with the origin mode (DECOM). vttest exercises these interactions extensively; most small DIY parsers fail this category first.

points.claim: G0/G1 character sets and VT100 line drawing are required for vim/neovim borders and are not trivial
points.detail: vim and neovim use the VT100 Special Graphics character set (G1, invoked via SO/SI or ESC ( 0) for box-drawing characters (window borders, horizontal/vertical separators) when in legacy mode. The G0/G1 system requires tracking four G-sets (G0–G3), two active slots (GL/GR), SO (Shift Out, 0x0E) and SI (Shift In, 0x0F) control codes, and ESC ( / ) / ESC * / ESC + designate sequences for each slot. SwiftTerm implements this in CharSets.swift (262 LOC) plus Terminal.swift lines 2530–2581 (setgCharset) and 1675–1682 (SO/SI handlers). The dutch 'ij' ligature edge case is even commented in the source (Terminal.swift line 1225). For a coding-only use-case this is a required, not optional, feature — neovim tree-sitter highlights and many TUI apps rely on it.

points.claim: Unicode width and grapheme clusters are the single hardest long-tail correctness problem and remain unsolved in every shipped terminal as of 2026
points.detail: The fundamental issue: terminals use a fixed cell grid; Unicode has multi-codepoint grapheme clusters (ZWJ sequences, regional indicators, variation selectors) where wcwidth() per-codepoint is wrong. Mitchell Hashimoto's detailed writeup ('Grapheme Clusters and Terminal Emulators') shows the farmer emoji (U+1F9D1 ZWJ U+1F33E) returns widths 2+0+2=4 per codepoint vs. the correct 2 for the cluster. Correct implementation requires UAX #29 (Unicode Text Segmentation) and stateful grapheme-break algorithm. Even Ghostty and kitty — the two highest-scoring terminals tested in the 2025 survey — do not correctly handle all cases. East Asian Ambiguous Width remains broken in Windows Terminal (issue #14702 still open). The jeffquast.com 2025 survey found cursor misalignment as the most common failure mode across all tested terminals. SwiftTerm uses Apple's Swift String grapheme segmentation (which is correct) but still needs to track cluster widths in a cell grid, stored in UnicodeWidthData.swift (316 LOC of generated tables). A DIY implementation will need to either vendor a UAX-29 grapheme segmentation library or use Swift's built-in .unicodeScalars/.characters view with east-asian-width tables. This is 2–4 engineer-weeks of correctness work alone.

points.claim: OSC codes required for a coding use-case: OSC 0/1/2 (window title), OSC 52 (clipboard — essential for remote vim), OSC 7 (cwd — used by shells), OSC 8 (hyperlinks) are each distinct string parsers
points.detail: OSC strings arrive as ESC ] <code> ; <data> BEL|ST. Each code requires a separate handler. OSC 52 (clipboard) is critical for remote vim sessions over SSH/WireGuard — it allows :y to yank into the local clipboard. OSC 7 (cwd) is used by iTerm2, fish, and Ghostty for tab titles and new-tab inheritance. OSC 8 (hyperlinks) is increasingly used by modern ls, git log, and error formatters. wezterm's osc.rs is 2,000 LOC just for the OSC semantic layer. SwiftTerm implements OSC 52 at Terminal.swift lines 1787–1814. OSC is straightforward to parse (scan until BEL or ST) but the sheer variety of codes means there are always more to add. For the coding subset: OSC 0/2/7/52 are the required ones; OSC 8 and iTerm2-style OSC 1337 are nice-to-have.

points.claim: Kitty keyboard protocol is required for nvim key disambiguation (Ctrl-M vs Enter, Ctrl-[ vs Esc, all modifier combos) and is moderately complex for the emulator side
points.detail: Without Kitty keyboard protocol, nvim cannot distinguish Ctrl-M from Enter, Ctrl-I from Tab, or Alt-modified keys from bare Escape sequences. The emulator must: (1) maintain independent mode stacks per screen (main/alt), (2) encode keydown/keyup/repeat events as CSI u sequences with modifier bitfields, (3) handle 5 progressive enhancement flags (disambiguate, report events, alternate keys, all-as-codes, associated text), (4) generate shifted/unshifted key variants. SwiftTerm implements this in KittyKeyboardProtocol.swift (53 LOC protocol) + KittyKeyboardEncoder.swift (904 LOC). xterm.js's KittyKeyboard.ts is 526 LOC. wezterm's kitty.rs is 979 LOC. This is a self-contained, well-specified feature that can be added incrementally — nvim works without it in legacy mode, so this can be deferred to iteration 2. Estimate: 1–2 engineer-weeks to implement correctly.

points.claim: Reflow on resize is a correctness requirement for any decent UX but adds 1–2 weeks of non-obvious complexity
points.detail: When the terminal width changes, wrapped lines should re-wrap at the new width rather than being truncated or corrupted. This requires each BufferLine to track a 'isWrapped' flag and the resize function to join wrapped runs back into logical lines before splitting them at the new width. SwiftTerm's Buffer.swift (1,255 LOC) tracks isWrapped per line and handles this in the resize path. Contour terminal has documented a 'line reflow mode' extension (as a DEC private mode toggle). A DIY implementation that ignores reflow will produce visually broken output whenever the user resizes the window — acceptable for a prototype but not for daily coding. Estimate: 1–2 weeks to get right.

points.claim: Kitty graphics protocol and Sixel are optional for vim/neovim/tmux/git — skip them in v1
points.detail: lazygit uses Kitty graphics for image previews in some configurations; btop can use Sixel or Kitty graphics for charts. But none of the core coding tools (vim, neovim, tmux, git, fzf) require graphics support for text operation. SwiftTerm has KittyGraphics.swift (1,936 LOC) and SixelDcsHandler.swift (403 LOC) — these are significant features but entirely deferrable. A v1 implementation that shows a 'graphics not supported' fallback remains fully usable. Ghostty took over a year to implement its Kitty graphics support after the initial terminal emulator was working.

points.claim: The 'not too complex' framing is partially accurate for the 80% case but brutally wrong for the long tail
points.detail: Ghostty was developed from 2021 as a side project by an experienced systems engineer (Mitchell Hashimoto) and was not publicly released until December 2024 — roughly 3 years of part-time then full-time work on what looks like 'just a terminal emulator'. xterm.js has been in active development since ~2014 with multiple full-time contributors and still has open grapheme-cluster correctness issues. The 80% coding use-case (vim/tmux/git working, colors correct, mouse working, alt-screen correct) is achievable in 8–12 engineer-weeks by a competent iOS/Swift engineer using SwiftTerm or libvterm as the VT state machine (not from scratch). DIY from scratch to the same 80% point is closer to 12–16 weeks. Getting from 80% to vttest-clean 95%+ adds another 8–16 weeks of long-tail bug fixing. The bugs at the long tail are not hard algorithmically but they require knowledge of obscure spec text, real-world app testing, and time.

points.claim: SwiftTerm's headless Terminal engine is the only pure-Swift, render-agnostic, mature VT state machine directly usable as library (A) in the architecture
points.detail: SwiftTerm's HeadlessTerminal.swift provides a Terminal subclass with no rendering or UI dependency. The Terminal class is render-agnostic: it calls delegate callbacks for damage notifications and cursor moves; the consumer (your Metal renderer) reads Terminal.buffer and CharData cells. SwiftTerm has 1,035 commits over 7 years, active maintenance as of 2025 (last PR merged within days per search results), and is already used in commercial SSH apps (Secure Shellfish, La Terminal, CodeEdit). License: MIT. It handles all the features enumerated above — including Kitty keyboard (904 LOC encoder), Sixel, OSC 52, grapheme clusters via Swift's native String — making it the ideal 'mature VT state library' for the architecture the user is considering, and directly usable without any Rust or Zig toolchain.

corpus.effort_estimate: (a) Good-enough-for-coding subset DIY in Swift (vim/tmux/git/lazygit working, truecolor, mouse, alt-screen, bracketed paste, DECSTBM, G0/G1 charset, Kitty keyboard): 12–16 engineer-weeks. Breakdown: state machine parser 1–2 wk, SGR + DEC private modes + cursor movement 3–4 wk, mouse protocols 1 wk, alt-screen + scroll regions 1–2 wk, G0/G1 charset 1 wk, grapheme/wide-char (80% correct) 2–3 wk, OSC 0/2/7/52 1 wk, Kitty keyboard 1–2 wk, reflow-on-resize 1 wk, debugging against real apps 2–3 wk. (b) Robust / vttest-clean (95%+ vttest pass, correct grapheme clustering per UAX-29, all DEC modes, DCS passthrough, VT52 compat, reflow correct): add 12–16 additional engineer-weeks of incremental correctness work, totaling 24–32 engineer-weeks. These estimates assume one experienced engineer, not a junior. Using SwiftTerm as the VT state machine (part A) instead of DIY reduces effort by roughly 60–70%: your engineering effort becomes implementing the Metal/UIKit rendering layer against SwiftTerm's damage callbacks, which is 4–8 engineer-weeks for rendering + integration, not terminal parsing.
claims_to_verify: SwiftTerm license is MIT — verify at https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/LICENSE
SwiftTerm HeadlessTerminal has no UIKit/AppKit dependency — verify by inspecting HeadlessTerminal.swift and checking import statements
SwiftTerm's grapheme cluster handling is correct for coding use-case (emoji, CJK, combining diacritics) — test against btop and nvim with CJK filenames

open_questions: Does SwiftTerm's Terminal engine expose a clean enough callback API for your use-case, or does it assume too much about buffer ownership? Review HeadlessTerminal.swift and the TerminalDelegate protocol before committing.

Does your use-case require tmux passthrough / DCS (Device Control Strings)? tmux uses DCS for its own protocol; if you intend to run tmux inside your client, DCS passthrough must be implemented correctly in the state layer.
Sixel / Kitty graphics: lazygit image previews use Kitty graphics. Is that a must-have for v1? If yes, add 3–4 weeks for Kitty graphics protocol.

What is the target TERM value you'll advertise to the host? Setting TERM=xterm-256color or TERM=ghostty affects which sequences applications send. Choosing a well-known value reduces the set of sequences you must handle.

How does your iOS client handle the keyboard? iOS software keyboard does not produce raw keystrokes for many modifier combos (Ctrl+key). The key-encoding problem (Kitty keyboard protocol) is secondary to the iOS input pipeline problem — you may need a custom key panel for coding use.

Is the neovim grapheme cluster mode 2027 (unicode_core) on the host side relevant? If the host nvim is configured for mode 2027, the client terminal must also support it or cursor positions will desync.
